title: Biotech news from around the world - Nature Biotechnology
gist: A two-item news roundup covers Stellenbosch University/Agricultural Research Council's CRISPR–Cas9-edited grapevine (Africa's first gene-edited grapevine, resistant to downy mildew and more water-efficient) and the GenomeIndia project, which sequenced 9,768 people across 83 populations to find 130 million genetic variants, nearly a third previously unreported globally.

insights.claim: Scientists from Stellenbosch University and South Africa's Agricultural Research Council used CRISPR–Cas9 to silence the VvDMR6.1 gene in grapevine, producing Africa's first gene-edited grapevine.
insights.speaker: Nature Biotechnology
insights.so_what: This is a proof point for CRISPR-based crop trait editing gaining ground on the African continent, relevant to agbiotech investors tracking regional regulatory openness to gene-edited crops.

insights.claim: Silencing the VvDMR6.1 gene simultaneously reduced the grapevine's vulnerability to downy mildew (a fungal disease) and increased the plant's ability to conserve water, delivering combined disease-resistance and drought-tolerance benefits from a single edit.
insights.speaker: Nature Biotechnology
insights.so_what: A single gene edit producing dual agronomic benefits (disease resistance plus water efficiency) strengthens the commercial case for gene-edited viticulture in water-stressed wine-growing regions.
insights.locator: reduced the grapevine’s vulnerability to downy mildew

insights.claim: The GenomeIndia genomic sequencing project, funded by India's Department of Biotechnology, sequenced the genomes of 9,768 healthy people from 83 populations and identified 130 million genetic variants, of which almost a third had never previously been reported in global scientific databases.
insights.speaker: Nature Biotechnology
insights.so_what: A large novel-variant catalogue specific to Indian populations creates a distinct pharmacogenomic dataset that drugmakers and diagnostics companies can license or build on for population-specific dosing and risk models, an asset largely missing from Western-biased reference genomes.

insights.claim: The GenomeIndia dataset revealed population-specific genetic risk factors including variants that affect how the body processes certain drugs, variants linked to anesthesia-related complications, and elevated levels of genetic homozygosity that can raise the risk of recessive genetic diseases.
insights.speaker: Nature Biotechnology
insights.so_what: Drug-metabolism and anesthesia-risk variants specific to Indian populations are directly actionable for pharmacogenomic dosing guidance and could inform clinical trial design and labeling for drugs marketed in India.
insights.locator: to reveal genetic risk factors in some populations
